does: This paper identifies and addresses a fundamental algorithmic bias in RLHF-based LLM alignment stemming from KL-divergence regularization. The authors demonstrate that standard RLHF can lead to preference collapse, where minority preferences are suppressed in favor of maximizing majority reward signals. They propose Preference Matching (PM) RLHF, which replaces the KL regularizer with a negative log-probability regularizer derived from optimization principles under the Bradley-Terry-Luce/Plackett-Luce preference model. This new regularizer provably aligns LLM outputs with the full preference distribution of the reward model rather than collapsing toward high-reward responses, maintaining response diversity while still pursuing reward maximization.
connects: This work builds on the established RLHF framework introduced by Christiano et al. and used widely in modern LLM alignment (GPT-3.5, Claude, etc.). It engages with longstanding concerns about KL-divergence regularization in reinforcement learning and connects to preference modeling literature using Bradley-Terry-Luce models, which have classical roots in ranking and choice theory. The paper extends recent critiques of RLHF's failure modes and responds to observed phenomena in practice where alignment procedures narrow output diversity. It also relates to broader concerns about fairness and statistical efficiency in machine learning decision-making systems, drawing connections between preference aggregation and algorithmic bias in neural network training.
enables: This work points toward more principled approaches to LLM alignment that preserve preference heterogeneity rather than concentrating on narrow reward optima. It suggests follow-up research into alternative regularization schemes that maintain distributional properties of human feedback. The theoretical framework using Plackett-Luce models could enable better preference elicitation and aggregation methods that respect minority viewpoints in multi-stakeholder settings. The approach could extend to other domains where AI systems must serve diverse user populations without sacrificing minority preferences, and may inform development of fairer ranking and recommendation systems that avoid algorithmic bias through regularization design.
